stem: Let \( p(x) \) be the polynomial of degree at most \( 2 \) that interpolates the three data points \( (1, [[y1]]) \), \( (2, [[y2]]) \), \( (3, [[y3]]) \). Such a \( p \) exists and is unique. What is \( p([[xq]]) \)?
solution: The three points determine a unique quadratic \( p(x) = [[A]]x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \) (its values at \( x = 1, 2, 3 \) are exactly \( [[y1]], [[y2]], [[y3]] \)). Evaluating at \( x = [[xq]] \) gives \( p([[xq]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This extrapolates linearly using only the last two points, ignoring the curvature the third point requires.
This extrapolates linearly using only the first two points, ignoring the quadratic term.
This just reports the nearest tabulated value \( y \) at \( x = 3 \) instead of interpolating.
This just reports the tabulated value \( y \) at \( x = 1 \) instead of interpolating.
This is off by two from the interpolated value.
This is off by three from the interpolated value.

stem: By the polynomial interpolation existence\(-\)uniqueness theorem, what is the minimum number of data points (with distinct \( x \)-values) needed to guarantee that there is exactly one interpolating polynomial of degree at most \( [[n]] \) through them?
solution: A polynomial of degree at most \( n \) has \( n+1 \) free coefficients, and \( n+1 \) points with distinct \( x \)-values give a linear system (Vandermonde) with a unique solution for those coefficients. So the minimum number of points is \( n + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This confuses the degree itself with the number of points needed.
This is one more point than actually required.
This doubles the required count, confusing it with some unrelated rule of thumb.
This doubles the degree and adds one, overcounting the required points.
This is one point short of what is required (the system would be underdetermined).
This overcounts the required number of points by three.

stem: For the interpolation nodes \( x_0 = [[x0]] \), \( x_1 = [[x1]] \), \( x_2 = [[x2]] \), the interpolating polynomial is constructed in Lagrange form as \( p(x) = y_0 L_0(x) + y_1 L_1(x) + y_2 L_2(x) \), where the basis polynomial associated with \( x_1 \) is \( L_1(x) = \dfrac{(x-x_0)(x-x_2)}{(x_1-x_0)(x_1-x_2)} \). What is \( L_1([[xq]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This computes \( L_2([[xq]]) \), the basis polynomial for node \( x_2 \), instead of \( L_1 \).
This computes \( L_0([[xq]]) \), the basis polynomial for node \( x_0 \), instead of \( L_1 \).
This adds the two denominator factors instead of multiplying them.
This subtracts the correct value from 1, as if it were a complementary weight.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct basis value.

stem: The unique polynomial \( p(x) = Ax^{2} + Bx + C \) of degree at most \( 2 \) passes through \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), \( ([[x1]], [[y1]]) \), \( ([[x2]], [[y2]]) \). Using the Newton divided-difference formula for the leading coefficient, \( A = \dfrac{y_0}{(x_0-x_1)(x_0-x_2)} + \dfrac{y_1}{(x_1-x_0)(x_1-x_2)} + \dfrac{y_2}{(x_2-x_0)(x_2-x_1)} \), find \( A \).
solution: Summing the three divided-difference terms gives the leading coefficient: \( A = \dfrac{[[y0]]}{([[x0]]-([[x1]]))([[x0]]-([[x2]]))} + \dfrac{[[y1]]}{([[x1]]-([[x0]]))([[x1]]-([[x2]]))} + \dfrac{[[y2]]}{([[x2]]-([[x0]]))([[x2]]-([[x1]]))} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the third (\( y_2 \)) term of the sum.
This keeps only the \( y_2 \) term and drops the other two.
This uses a bogus averaging formula instead of the divided-difference sum.
This keeps only the middle (\( y_1 \)) term and drops the other two.
This is one more than the correct leading coefficient.
This is one less than the correct leading coefficient.
